texts.orig: Post combustion carbon dioxide capture raises tremendous chemical engineering challenges. For the first generation of industrial installations, gas liquid absorption in chemical solvents realized in packed columns is classically considered to be the best available technology (1). Two major challenges have however to be met in order to achieve technico-economical targets: decrease the energy requirement of the process through novel solvents or heat integration approaches and decrease the size of the installation through process intensification.
texts.text: Post combustion carbon dioxide capture raises tremendous chemical engineering challenges. For the first generation of industrial installations, gas liquid absorption in chemical solvents realized in packed columns is classically considered to be the best available technology (1). Two major challenges have however to be met in order to achieve technico-economical targets: decrease the energy requirement of the process through novel solvents or heat integration approaches and decrease the size of the installation through process intensification.

texts.orig: A promising strategy for intensification of the gas-liquid absorption step is the use of hollow fiber membrane contactors instead of packed columns. Membrane gas–liquid contactors make use of a porous polymeric membrane to separate the gas and liquid phase. Ideally, the micro-pores of the hydrophobic membrane are completely gas filled. Hence, the membranes themselves do not offer any selectivity for the gases to be separated; this role is fulfilled by the absorption liquid. The specific interfacial area for mass transfer in a membrane gas–liquid contactor is the membrane surface area per contactor volume. For commercially available hollow fiber membrane modules, it varies between 1500–3000 m

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years, the efficiency of membrane contactor for CO2 capture has been extensively studied (4; 5; 6; 7) including materials, mass transfer as well as process design issues, but a rigorous evaluation of their effective potential in terms of intensification is still lacking. Moreover, controversial results have been reported such as intensification factors, compared to packed columns, ranging between 10 and 0.8 on a total unit volume basis. This unclear situation results from different factors. First, experimental comparison of membrane contactors versus packed absorption columns performances is seldom. Second, the evaluation of membrane contactors is generally performed at laboratory scale, under operating conditions which do not necessarily reflect industrial operation (i.e. fresh amine solutions are used, very high or very low capture ratios are achieved). These conditions have obviously to be reconsidered if a realistic comparison for industrial operation is aimed. In addition, pressure drop levels, which are known to be small for packed columns (typically 50 mbar on the gas side for an industrial packed column), have to be considered in order to minimize the energy impact of the process.

texts.orig: Process models of different complexities have been developed (6). The simplest models define an average overall mass transfer coefficient. The principle assumption of these models is the invariance of the gas, the membrane and the liquid side conductance all over the fiber length. Even though these models may be valid for particular operation conditions (i.e. a large excess of liquid-side reactant in the process), they cannot be applied to industrial operating conditions, where the liquid-side conductance varies dramatically over the reactor length, as shown in the following. Recently two-dimensional models were proposed, based on the resolution of the convective diffusion equations coupled to the chemical reaction in a cylindrical geometry (8) (9) (10) (11) (12). In general these models are used to determine selected parameters in particular situations, to find optimal, industrially relevant operating parameters. to fill the gap between very simple and quite complex models with a one-dimensional engineering-type model of a somehow intermediate complexity. odel predictions are used to determine and to optimize the most important process parameters.

texts.orig: The paper is structured as follows. First, some basic model assumptions as well as the parametric domain are exposed. The hydrodynamics of the gas as well as of the liquid flow are then discussed, leading to the choice of mass transfer relations. Assumptions relative to the modeling of the reactive diffusion in the liquid-side boundary layer are argued in section four. In section five, the variation of the local mass flux of the gaseous reactant is discussed in detail. Finally, in section six, the global characteristics of the hollow fiber membrane contactor are derived and discussed.

In section five, the variation of the local mass flux of the gaseous reactant is discussed in detail. Finally, in section six, the global characteristics of the hollow fiber membrane contactor are derived and discussed.

Hydrophobic porous membranes with uniform pore size and wetting characteristics are considered. he system is assumed to be isothermal, at steady state and in perfect counter-current plug flow. The volumetric liquid flow is considered to be invariant, whereas the volumetric gas flow varies with the pressure and the carbon dioxide depletion. The external and the internal liquid flow configuration are analyzed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In laminar flow through a cylindrical pipe, the convective heat and mass transfer coefficients can be estimated by the integration of the so-called Graetz equation for suitable boundary conditions (22). For low Graetz numbers, at the pipe entrance, the convective-diffusion boundary layer is under development, and the Sherwood number decreases with the distance from the pipe entrance. For sufficiently high Graetz numbers (Gz>0.03), the radial concentration profile is fully developed, and the Sherwood number attains a limiting value. Under these conditions, the mass transfer coefficient is solely a function of the diffusion coefficient and of the hydraulic flow diameter and does not depend on the flow velocity. Fully developed concentration profiles are frequently encountered in submillimetric systems, as the diffusion distances are small.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The physical mass transfer process in hollow fiber membrane modules has been discussed in detail in literature (4). An overview of available mass transfer correlations is given in (23). The mass transfer for the liquid flowing around the fibers is actually a three dimensional problem as the interaction of the different fibers should be taken into account. A two dimensional approximation suggested frequently is flow through an annulus characterized by the hydraulic diameter of the external flow section (26). Numerical solutions for the three dimensional formulation were presented for heat transfer in laminar flow for a fluid flowing axially between regular arrays of cylinders with a uniform surface temperature (27) or with a uniform wall heat flux (28). The numerical results clearly corroborate the existence of a limiting (or asymptotic) Nusselt number for sufficiently high thermal Graetz numbers. The analogy between heat and mass transfer permits to exploit these results in order to estimate the limiting Sherwood numbers that one would obtain in laminar mass transfer with uniform mass flux or uniform wall concentrations in the same geometry.
texts.text: The physical mass transfer process in hollow fiber membrane modules has been discussed in detail in literature (4). An overview of available mass transfer correlations is given in (23). The mass transfer for the liquid flowing around the fibers is actually a three dimensional problem as the interaction of the different fibers should be taken into account. A two dimensional approximation suggested frequently is flow through an annulus characterized by the hydraulic diameter of the external flow section (26). Numerical solutions for the three dimensional formulation were presented for heat transfer in laminar flow for a fluid flowing axially between regular arrays of cylinders with a uniform surface temperature (27) or with a uniform wall heat flux (28). The numerical results clearly corroborate the existence of a limiting (or asymptotic) Nusselt number for sufficiently high thermal Graetz numbers. The analogy between heat and mass transfer permits to exploit these results in order to estimate the limiting Sherwood numbers that one would obtain in laminar mass transfer with uniform mass flux or uniform wall concentrations in the same geometry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure A.1 depicts the variation of the limiting Sherwood number obtained by numerical simulation as a function of the bundle compacity for regular arrays of cylinders (27) (28). The two investigated limiting conditions, the uniform wall concentration (temperature in the original paper) and the uniform flux to the wall are shown. The Sherwood number is defined in Figure A.1 with respect to the hydraulic flow diameter of the external flow section (see Table 2) which differs from the original papers where the corresponding Nusselt number is defined with respect to the diameter of the cylinders. The wall condition has only a slight influence on the limiting Sherwood number. The influence of the geometry of the arrangement is much more important: triangular arrays clearly lead to higher Sherwood numbers and thus to higher mass transfer coefficients for a given hydraulic diameter and a given bundle compacity.

In addition, irregular arrays may lead to substantial maldistribution of the fluid, diminishing the overall efficiency of mass transfer in the bundle (4).

texts.orig: Figure A.1 shows the limiting values of the Sherwood number obtained for developed laminar cylindrical pipe flow for respectively a uniform wall concentration and a uniform wall flux. Again, the hydraulic flow diameter is used. For a bundle compacity comprised between 0.55 and 0.7, the pipe flow Sherwood numbers are sandwiched between the limiting values obtained numerically in the regular array geometries. For a bundle compacity of 0.6, as applied in this work, the limiting Sherwood number for pipe flow seems thus to be a reasonably correct estimation of the local limiting mass transfer coefficient.

texts.text: 13 Appendix B : Validation of the assumptions on flow hydrodynamics and mass transfer

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The validation of the hydrodynamics and mass transfer characteristics of the gas and the liquid flow is done using the reactor characteristics attained neglecting eventual gas-side mass transfer limitations. These conditions are cautious as they lead to the lowest possible residence times, and the highest gas and liquid velocities and thus to the lowest possible Graetz numbers and the highest possible Reynolds numbers.
texts.text: The validation of the hydrodynamics and mass transfer characteristics of the gas and the liquid flow is done using the reactor characteristics attained neglecting eventual gas-side mass transfer limitations. These conditions are cautious as they lead to the lowest possible residence times, and the highest gas and liquid velocities and thus to the lowest possible Graetz numbers and the highest possible Reynolds numbers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figures B.1a and B.1b illustrate the variation of the gas-phase Reynolds and Graetz number respectively with the fiber radius (see Table 4 for the definitions). The internal and the external liquid-flow configurations are illustrated. The maximum gas-phase Reynolds number calculated is 475 for external liquid-flow through a 1 mm radius fiber and a relative membrane thickness of 0.2 (see Fig. B.1a). As the gas-flow flows in this case inside the fibers, the flow regime can still be considered to be laminar, validating the assumptions made on gas-side mass transfer and pressure drop relations. The maximum gas-phase Reynolds number for internal liquid-flow, and external gas flow, is about 70, which is again sufficiently low to validate the assumptions on the laminar flow regime and on the pressure drop correlation in the porous medium created by the fiber bundle (24). The Graetz numbers (Fig. B.1b) are estimated for the final reactor length (z=Z). The gas-side Graetz number attained for the internal liquid flow configuration is about two times higher than that attained for the external flow configuration. In all cases, the gas-side Graetz numbers are significantly higher than unity thus validating the assumption of a developed gas-side concentration profile in the membrane contactor and leading to a constant Sherwood number in the estimation of the gas-side mass transfer coefficient (see Table 4).
texts.text: Figures B.1a and B.1b illustrate the variation of the gas-phase Reynolds and Graetz number respectively with the fiber radius (see Table 4 for the definitions). The internal and the external liquid-flow configurations are illustrated. The maximum gas-phase Reynolds number calculated is 475 for external liquid-flow through a 1 mm radius fiber and a relative membrane thickness of 0.2 (see Fig. B.1a). As the gas-flow flows in this case inside the fibers, the flow regime can still be considered to be laminar, validating the assumptions made on gas-side mass transfer and pressure drop relations. The maximum gas-phase Reynolds number for internal liquid-flow, and external gas flow, is about 70, which is again sufficiently low to validate the assumptions on the laminar flow regime and on the pressure drop correlation in the porous medium created by the fiber bundle (24). The Graetz numbers (Fig. B.1b) are estimated for the final reactor length (z=Z). The gas-side Graetz number attained for the internal liquid flow configuration is about two times higher than that attained for the external flow configuration. In all cases, the gas-side Graetz numbers are significantly higher than unity thus validating the assumption of a developed gas-side concentration profile in the membrane contactor and leading to a constant Sherwood number in the estimation of the gas-side mass transfer coefficient (see Table 4).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ratio between the liquid-side and the gas-side Reynolds and Graetz numbers depends on the ratio between the superficial velocities of the two phases as well as on the physico-chemical and geometrical system characteristics. The corresponding expressions are reported in Table 4. It is noteworthy that these relations are independent on the external fiber radius and on the membrane permeability. As the liquid to gas superficial velocity ratio is fixed by the operational constraints (the actual value is 0.00459), the ratio between the hydrodynamic characteristics of the two phases can easily be computed. It turns out that the liquid-side Reynolds numbers for the liquid internal and external flow configurations are at least respectively 4 and 75 times lower than the gas-side Reynolds numbers. These conditions lead to laminar liquid flow over the whole investigated parameter range.
texts.text: The ratio between the liquid-side and the gas-side Reynolds and Graetz numbers depends on the ratio between the superficial velocities of the two phases as well as on the physico-chemical and geometrical system characteristics. The corresponding expressions are reported in Table 4. It is noteworthy that these relations are independent on the external fiber radius and on the membrane permeability. As the liquid to gas superficial velocity ratio is fixed by the operational constraints (the actual value is 0.00459), the ratio between the hydrodynamic characteristics of the two phases can easily be computed. It turns out that the liquid-side Reynolds numbers for the liquid internal and external flow configurations are at least respectively 4 and 75 times lower than the gas-side Reynolds numbers. These conditions lead to laminar liquid flow over the whole investigated parameter range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The liquid-phase Graetz numbers are as well significantly lower than the gas-phase Graetz numbers. For the two liquid flow configurations (internal and external), the Graetz number at the flow outlet section varies between 0.2 for 1 mm radius fibers and about 0.4 for 0.2 mm radius fibers. These values are at least seven times higher than the limiting Graetz number necessary in order to consider a constant Sherwood number in the estimation of the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (i.e. 0.03, see Table 4). Thus the calculation procedure underestimates the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient in a relatively limited liquid entry section. However, the assumption of a constant liquid side mass transfer coefficient is cautious as it leads to a slight underestimation of the overall mass transfer conductance.

table_cells.text:
The gas phase is a mixture of saturated air (H2O+N2+O2) and carbon dioxide. 
The liquid phase is an aqueous solution of MEA.

The overall volumetric flow of the saturated air (H2O+N2+O2) varies with the gas pressure following the ideal gas law.
The overall volumetric flow of the liquid phase is constant.
The gas and the liquid are in perfect plug flow. The flow distribution is uniform, maldistribution effects are neglected.

The system is at steady state.
Only counter-current operation is envisioned as it is in most practical cases more efficient. 
Both, the external and the internal liquid-flow configuration are discussed.

Only hydrophobic porous membranes are considered, resulting in gas diffusion through the membrane. The gas-liquid interface is thus always located on the liquid-flow side of the fiber bundle.
Pore size and wetting characteristics are uniform throughout the membrane.
No solute transport occurs through the non-porous parts of the membrane.
Equilibrium exists at the gas-liquid interface and is described by Henry’s law. The Henry constant is unvarying over the concentration range of interest.

The curvature of the gas-liquid interface does not significantly affect the rate of mass transfer or the interfacial area.
The mass transfer is described adequately by simple film-type mass transfer coefficients.
The total internal or external membrane area is used as the gas-liquid interfacial area through which the transport takes place.
